summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that an unauthenticated attacker can read arbitrary files from the server via path traversal in /api/download. The PoC sends a GET request with '....//' sequences to traverse out of the reports directory and read /etc/passwd.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should cause the FUXA server to serve the contents of /etc/passwd in the HTTP response body, demonstrating arbitrary file read via path traversal.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh started the FUXA server, sent the crafted GET /api/download request with the '....//' bypass payload, and the server responded with the contents of /etc/passwd, confirming arbitrary file read.
verification_method: The verify script checks that the HTTP response body contains the string 'root:' which is the expected content of /etc/passwd on Linux. This distinguishes successful path traversal from a generic error response, file-not-found message, or startup failure.
evidence_produced_by_run: The FUXA server started successfully and responded to HTTP requests on port 1881.
The crafted GET /api/download request with '....//' path traversal returned HTTP 200.
The response body contained 'root:' confirming that /etc/passwd was read and served.
No authentication token or credentials were required.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned version, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
